input.headline: Olympic gold medallist gives 5 key tips for older swimmers returning to the pool

raw.headline: Olympic gold medallist gives 5 key tips for older swimmers returning to the pool

winner: I tried following Olympic gold medallist's tips to get back in the pool, but between the "perfect form" advice and the "don't hold your breath" life advice, I just ended up with a case of the flops—turns out I'm not ready for gold, just the float lane.

candidates.text: I asked an Olympic gold medallist for his top tip for older swimmers and he said, "Don't try to do a backstroke on your 80th birthday—just stick to floating like a cork.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Surprising and specific twist with a clear reference to age and swimming technique, delivered in a concise and natural way."}

candidates.text: I tried following Olympic gold medallist's tips to get back in the pool, but between the "perfect form" advice and the "don't hold your breath" life advice, I just ended up with a case of the flops—turns out I'm not ready for gold, just the float lane.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Puns on 'flops' and 'float lane' add surprise and specificity; concise and naturally funny with a relatable twist."}

candidates.text: Michael Phelps’ top tip? Don’t worry about getting back into shape—just remember your lane markings. You’re 38 now, so it’s less ‘swimming for gold’ and more ‘fighting the chlorine burns while pretending you’re still in your prime.’
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.0, "reason": "The joke is concise, specific, and surprising with a clever twist on age and swimming. It naturally ties into the headline and uses a well-known figure (Michael Phelps) for relatability and humor."}

candidates.text: She said the first tip is to avoid 'lapping yourself'—but she didn’t mention the part about lapping everyone else’s age group.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "The joke is concise, surprising, and specific, with a clever twist on the phrase 'lapping yourself' that ties back to the headline. It's naturally phrased and funny without being offensive or generic."}

candidates.text: Swimming again after retirement is simple—just remember: float before you try to race, check your hearing (you'll need both ears for lane rules), avoid comparing yourself to 20-year-olds, keep a towel handy for both sweat and ego, and most importantly, if you sink immediately, it's probably not because the pool changed.
